title: Bank Statement Analysis for Staffing Agencies and Temp Firms
meta: Staffing agencies have high revenue pass-through for worker wages. Learn how lenders correctly analyze staffing agency bank statements and avoid misreading gross billing as profit.
content:
The Staffing Agency Bank Statement Problem

Staffing agencies present one of the most commonly misanalyzed bank statement patterns in business lending. The reason: gross billing volumes are very high, but the vast majority of those funds immediately flow back out as worker wages. Lenders who don't apply an appropriate expense factor will dramatically overestimate profitability.

How Staffing Agency Cash Flows Work

A staffing agency bills clients at a "bill rate" (e.g., $25/hour) and pays workers at a "pay rate" (e.g., $18/hour). The spread ($7/hour in this example) is the gross margin, from which the agency pays overhead and profit. Typical gross margins in staffing are 20–35%.

On a bank statement, this looks like:

  • Weekly client invoice payments: Large deposits from client companies paying their weekly staffing invoices
  • Weekly payroll outflows: Almost equally large ACH debits for payroll processing — same week, often same day

A staffing firm billing $500,000/month will show $500,000 in deposits and ~$375,000 in payroll outflows. Their actual gross profit is only $125,000 — not $500,000.

The Correct Analysis Approach

Lenders must apply an expense factor that accounts for payroll pass-through. For staffing companies:

  • Identify the payroll processor outflows (ADP, Paychex, Gusto, direct payroll ACH)
  • Calculate net deposits after payroll costs
  • Apply additional overhead factor (overhead beyond payroll: office, benefits, recruiting costs)
  • Or use a flat 20–35% of gross deposits as qualifying income

StatementScrub calculates both gross deposits and net cash flow — showing the actual net position after all outflows, which gives lenders the right starting point for staffing company analysis.

What Lenders Look For

  • Client payment consistency — are invoice payments arriving regularly?
  • Payroll regularity — consistent payroll cycles confirm active placements
  • Net cash flow trend — is the business retaining income after payroll?
  • NSF events — a staffing company with NSF events has serious cash management problems (payroll must be met)
  • Client concentration — dependence on 1–2 major clients is a risk

Common Loan Needs

  • Accounts receivable financing / invoice factoring — most common in staffing due to 30–60 day invoice payment cycles vs. weekly payroll obligations
  • Working capital loans for rapid headcount expansion
  • SBA loans for larger agency acquisitions or office expansions

Related: Understanding cash flow ratios | Bank statement analysis and credit risk

Bottom Line

Staffing agency bank statements require an expense factor that accounts for payroll pass-through. Gross deposits are a misleading income figure in this industry — net margin analysis is essential for accurate credit assessment. Lenders who understand the staffing business model will correctly evaluate these companies; those who don't will either overextend or incorrectly deny creditworthy agencies.
